analysis_metadata.task: Priority 2 Memory Table Migration v19 - Production Readiness Assessment
analysis_metadata.schema_change: ALTER TABLE memories ADD COLUMN account_id TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'default'

critical_gaps.title: Partial Migration - Schema Added But Backfill Incomplete
critical_gaps.description: If migration v19 completes schema addition (line 652) but fails during backfill (lines 657-668), the database enters an inconsistent state where account_id exists but many rows still have 'default' instead of their true tenant ID from metadata.
critical_gaps.why_it_matters: In production, queries filtering by account_id would exclude tenant data until migration is re-run or manually fixed. This violates tenant isolation guarantees.
what_could_go_wrong: Operator runs migration, network/disk error occurs during UPDATE (line 657-668)
Process crashes mid-backfill, leaving some rows with 'default', others with real tenant IDs
Multi-process environment attempts concurrent migration, one succeeds, one fails
Tenant A's memory is invisible to Tenant A queries if stuck in 'default' after partial migration
critical_gaps.test_scenario: Simulate partial migration: execute ALTER TABLE at line 652, then trigger failure mid-UPDATE. Verify database is left in detectable bad state and operator can re-run recovery safely.
critical_gaps.test_code_pattern: Mock rusqlite::Connection to fail after first UPDATE (line 657) completes but before second UPDATE (line 664). Verify idempotency: running migrate_v19 again should complete backfill correctly.
critical_gaps.recommendation: Generate test: test_migration_v19_partial_failure_is_recoverable - verify second run of migrate_v19 completes backfill

critical_gaps.title: Concurrent Migration Execution - Race Condition on Schema Check
critical_gaps.description: Line 650 checks `column_exists()` then line 652 executes ALTER. If two processes run migrate_v19 simultaneously, both may pass the check before either executes ALTER, causing duplicate column creation error.
critical_gaps.why_it_matters: In multi-process deployments (workers, multiple app instances), concurrent migrations could crash the entire service if not handled.
what_could_go_wrong: Worker A checks column_exists() = false, Worker B checks column_exists() = false
Both attempt ALTER TABLE simultaneously
SQLite locks prevent ALTER, one succeeds, one fails with 'duplicate column'
Migration rollback/error handling leaves database in unknown state
critical_gaps.test_scenario: Simulate concurrent migration: two threads both check column_exists() before either executes ALTER. Verify the first succeeds and second gracefully detects column exists and continues.
critical_gaps.test_code_pattern: Use parking_lot::Mutex or std::sync::mpsc to orchestrate two migration threads. Patch column_exists() to return false for both before ALTER executes. Verify both complete successfully.
critical_gaps.recommendation: Generate test: test_migration_v19_concurrent_execution_safe - verify both concurrent runs complete without ALTER conflict

critical_gaps.title: count_all() Leaks All Tenants' Memory Count - No account_id Filter
critical_gaps.description: Function at line 843-863 queries ALL memories across ALL accounts without tenant filtering. count_all(Some('episodic')) returns aggregated count for all tenants' episodic memories, violating tenant isolation.
critical_gaps.why_it_matters: If count_all() is used in billing, quota enforcement, or analytics, Tenant A learns Tenant B's memory usage. Compliance violation (multi-tenancy data leakage).
what_could_go_wrong: API endpoint /api/memory/stats calls count_all() and exposes global memory count
Billing system uses count_all() to charge all tenants equally based on total count
Tenant A can infer Tenant B's activity level by calling count_all() repeatedly
No account_id parameter in function signature, impossible to fix without breaking API
critical_gaps.test_scenario: Create memories for Tenant A and Tenant B. Call count_all('episodic'). Verify return is 2. Then test that if used in tenant-aware context, it fails isolation tests.
critical_gaps.test_code_pattern: Setup two semantic stores (or same store with different metadata). Insert 5 memories for 'tenant-a', 3 for 'tenant-b'. Assert count_all('episodic') returns 8 (not filtered by account). THEN verify this is NOT called from tenant-aware endpoints.
critical_gaps.recommendation: Accept risk + mitigate: Verify count_all() is NEVER called from tenant-aware API paths. Add #[deprecated] warning: "Use count_by_account() instead for tenant queries". Generate test: test_count_all_is_not_exposed_via_api

critical_gaps.title: recall_via_vector_store() Missing account_id Filter in Delegation
critical_gaps.description: Line 1150 builds SQL for embedding search but does NOT pass account_id to filter object before delegating to external VectorStore (line 206). The query_embedding path delegates to external backend which may not enforce tenant isolation.
critical_gaps.why_it_matters: If VectorStore backend (Weaviate, Qdrant, Pinecone) is used, tenant A could query and retrieve Tenant B's embeddings if backend isn't tenant-aware.
what_could_go_wrong: External VectorStore is shared across tenants but unaware of account_id
Vector similarity search returns results from other tenants
Operator doesn't realize VectorStore needs separate index per tenant
No test validates account_id is passed to VectorStore during recall_with_embedding
critical_gaps.test_scenario: Mock VectorStore backend. Call recall_with_embedding() with filter={account_id: 'tenant-a'}. Verify account_id is passed to VectorStore.query() call (line 206).
critical_gaps.test_code_pattern: Create MockVectorStore implementing VectorStore trait. Track which parameters are passed to its query() method. Assert account_id from filter is included in query call.
critical_gaps.recommendation: Generate test: test_recall_via_vector_store_includes_account_id_filter - verify account_id is passed to external VectorStore backend

critical_gaps.title: get_by_id() No Tenant Validation - Returns Any Tenant's Memory
critical_gaps.description: Function at line 496-520 retrieves memory by ID without account_id parameter or filter. Tenant A can call get_by_id(memory_uuid) and retrieve ANY tenant's memory if UUID is known.
critical_gaps.why_it_matters: Direct ID-based access bypasses all tenant isolation. Severe data leakage vulnerability.
what_could_go_wrong: Tenant A guesses/brute-forces memory UUID from another tenant
get_by_id() is called without tenant context check in API layer
Memory UUID could be guessable (sequential, predictable UUIDs)
No test validates get_by_id() rejects cross-tenant access
critical_gaps.test_scenario: Create memory for Tenant A. Call get_by_id(memory_id) without providing Tenant A context. Verify it returns the memory (current behavior is WRONG). Then add account_id parameter and verify without account_id it fails.
critical_gaps.test_code_pattern: Store memory for 'tenant-a'. Call get_by_id(id, None, include_deleted=false). Assert it returns memory (current bug). THEN test that a tenant-aware wrapper would correctly reject this.
critical_gaps.recommendation: BLOCKING: Add account_id parameter to get_by_id() signature. Update all callers to pass tenant context. Generate test: test_get_by_id_with_account_id_isolation

critical_gaps.title: DEFAULT_ACCOUNT_ID = 'default' Collides With Real Tenant Named 'default'
critical_gaps.description: Lines 7 and 21 define DEFAULT_ACCOUNT_ID = 'default'. If an operator creates a real tenant named 'default', all legacy rows (backfilled to 'default') become indistinguishable from 'default' tenant's actual rows.
critical_gaps.why_it_matters: Two different entities claim same account_id. Queries for 'default' tenant return mixed data from both legacy+real.
what_could_go_wrong: Operator creates tenant with org name 'default' (e.g., Default Organization)
Legacy rows and new rows both use account_id='default'
count_by_account('default', None) returns combined count
Impossible to distinguish legacy data from 'default' tenant's actual data
critical_gaps.test_scenario: Insert legacy memory without explicit tenant. Backfill assigns account_id='default'. Then insert new memory with account_id='default' (real tenant). Query count_by_account('default'). Verify we can't separate them (demonstrates the problem).
critical_gaps.test_code_pattern: Simulate two sources: legacy row → backfilled to 'default', new row → inserted with account_id='default'. Assert count_by_account('default') returns 2. Then verify no way to distinguish which is which.
critical_gaps.recommendation: Generate test: test_default_account_id_collision_documented - verify behavior is documented. Optionally: use UUID-based default (e.g., 'system-00000000') instead of string 'default'

critical_gaps.title: Insert During Migration - Does DEFAULT Correctly Assign New Rows?
critical_gaps.description: If row is inserted DURING v19 migration (while backfill UPDATE is running), the DEFAULT 'default' is applied. But if metadata.account_id is present, it's not synchronized until next backfill run.
critical_gaps.why_it_matters: Memory inserted mid-migration might have account_id='default' even if metadata.account_id='tenant-a', causing it to be invisible to tenant queries until next migration run.
what_could_go_wrong: Agent inserts memory at line 159 while migration UPDATE is running
DEFAULT applies 'default' to account_id column
metadata contains account_id but physical column is 'default'
Tenant A's newest memory is invisible because account_id='default'
critical_gaps.test_scenario: Start migration v19. Pause after schema created (line 652) but before backfill (line 657). Insert new memory with metadata.account_id='tenant-a'. Resume migration. Verify new memory's account_id is correctly set to 'tenant-a' (not 'default').
critical_gaps.test_code_pattern: Use parking_lot::Mutex to coordinate: migrate_v19 acquires lock, pauses after ALTER. Insert memory in separate thread. Resume migrate_v19. Query new memory's account_id. Assert it's 'tenant-a' (requires backfill of metadata.account_id).
critical_gaps.recommendation: Generate test: test_insert_during_v19_migration_syncs_account_id - verify inserts mid-migration are backfilled correctly

critical_gaps.title: Metadata.account_id Deserialization Error - Corrupt JSON Silently Defaults to 'default'
critical_gaps.description: Lines 23-30 extract account_id from metadata JSON. If metadata is corrupt (malformed JSON in database), json_extract() on line 658 silently returns NULL, then second UPDATE (line 664) applies DEFAULT 'default'. No error logged.
critical_gaps.why_it_matters: Corrupt metadata is masked. Tenant's data defaults to 'default' without operator visibility.
what_could_go_wrong: Old schema stored invalid JSON in metadata column
SQLite json_extract() returns NULL on malformed JSON
Backfill silently assigns 'default' to corrupt rows
No audit log or warning that data was potentially reassigned
Operator doesn't know which rows were corrupt
critical_gaps.test_scenario: Insert memory with corrupt metadata (invalid JSON string). Run v19 migration. Verify account_id is set to 'default' (not error). Check if any warning/log is emitted (should be).
critical_gaps.test_code_pattern: Insert row with metadata='invalid json string' directly via SQL. Run migrate_v19(). Query account_id. Assert it's 'default'. THEN verify a log warning was emitted (if not, generate test that one should be).
critical_gaps.recommendation: Generate test: test_v19_migration_handles_corrupt_metadata_with_warning - verify corrupt rows are logged as warnings

critical_gaps.title: Operator Runbook Missing: Recovery Steps for Failed Migration
critical_gaps.description: Migration v19 doesn't document failure scenarios or recovery steps. If backfill fails (network, disk full, timeout), operator has no runbook to recover without data loss.
critical_gaps.why_it_matters: In production, failed migration could cause downtime until operator manually fixes database state.
what_could_go_wrong: Migration starts, UPDATE (line 657) times out after processing 50% of rows
Operator has no guidance on what state database is in
Manual recovery steps are unclear - should they re-run migration? Drop column? Restore from backup?
Multiple operators applying different fixes simultaneously causes inconsistency
critical_gaps.test_scenario: Generate migration failure scenario documentation. Verify each recovery step in the runbook works (e.g., 'if backfill hangs, run migrate_v19 again').
critical_gaps.test_code_pattern: Document runbook: 'V19 Migration Failure Recovery'. For each step (partial schema, backfill timeout, etc.), write a test that simulates failure and verifies recovery step resolves it.
critical_gaps.recommendation: Generate documentation test + code: test_migration_v19_failure_scenarios_documented - verify runbook covers all edge cases

critical_gaps.title: lowest_confidence() Missing account_id - Memory Eviction Could Delete Other Tenant's Data
critical_gaps.description: Function at line 808-840 queries lowest-confidence memories for agent eviction (line 820). Does NOT filter by account_id. If two tenants share an agent_id, evicting lowest_confidence could delete other tenant's memories.
critical_gaps.why_it_matters: Cross-tenant data deletion. Tenant A's eviction action deletes Tenant B's lowest-confidence memories if agent is shared.
what_could_go_wrong: Agent ID is reused across tenants (UUID collision or intentional)
lowest_confidence() returns memories from both tenants
forget_memory() deletes memories from wrong tenant
Tenant A's quota enforcement deletes Tenant B's data
critical_gaps.test_scenario: Create agent_id shared by two tenants. Insert 5 memories for tenant-a, 5 for tenant-b (same agent_id). Call lowest_confidence(agent_id, 3). Verify returned IDs belong to BOTH tenants (demonstrating isolation failure).
critical_gaps.test_code_pattern: Use same agent_id for two accounts. Insert memories with different account_id values. Query lowest_confidence(). Assert results are filtered by... nothing (current bug). Then add account_id filter.
critical_gaps.recommendation: Generate test: test_lowest_confidence_does_not_cross_tenant_boundary - add account_id parameter to function OR add test that demonstrates this is only called with tenant context

recommended_priority_order.title: BLOCKING: Fix get_by_id() Tenant Isolation
recommended_priority_order.rationale: Critical security vulnerability - any tenant can read any other tenant's memory by UUID

recommended_priority_order.title: Migration Reliability: Partial Failure & Concurrency
recommended_priority_order.rationale: Production crashes or data loss in multi-process deployments

recommended_priority_order.title: Query Isolation: count_all(), VectorStore, lowest_confidence()
recommended_priority_order.rationale: Data leakage and cross-tenant contamination in core query paths

recommended_priority_order.title: Operational: Runbook & Recovery
recommended_priority_order.rationale: Enables fast MTTR if migration fails in production

risk_assessment.recommended_action: DO NOT DEPLOY until gaps 1, 2, 5 are resolved. Strongly recommend resolving gaps 3, 4, 10 before production use.
